title: Flechtwerk — Observability
description: Flechtwerk framework metrics across all example stages: throughput, latency, extractor poll cycles, MQTT, config store, transformer tasks & state restore, process health. Local adaptation of the production Flechtwerk Observability dashboard.

panels.description: Count of scrape targets responding (up == 1) under the flechtwerk job. Targets stay down until you start the matching example — that is expected.
panels.title: Active Stages (up)

panels.description: Sum of flechtwerk_active_configs across selected extractors.
panels.title: Active Configs (sum)

panels.description: Sum of flechtwerk_config_store_entries — the "did my config arrive?" gauge.
panels.title: Config Store Entries (sum)

panels.description: Sum of flechtwerk_tasks_assigned — transformer tasks (input partitions) owned across all instances.
panels.title: Tasks Assigned (sum)

panels.description: rate(flechtwerk_transactions_committed_total[5m]) summed across selected transformers.
panels.title: Tx Commits / sec

panels.description: Extractor poll cycles completed per second across the selected examples. (Replaces the production working-set-memory stat — stages run on the host here, so there is no cAdvisor.)
panels.title: Poll Cycles / sec

panels.description: histogram_quantile(0.95) of flechtwerk_message_processing_seconds — excludes Kafka I/O.

targets.expr: histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (client_id, le) (rate(flechtwerk_message_processing_seconds_bucket{example=~"$example", stage=~"$stage", client_id=~"$client_id"}[5m])))

panels.title: Message processing — p95 (user code)

targets.expr: histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (client_id, le) (rate(flechtwerk_batch_processing_seconds_bucket{example=~"$example", stage=~"$stage", client_id=~"$client_id"}[5m])))

panels.title: Batch processing — p95 (incl. Kafka tx commit, transformers)

targets.expr: histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (client_id, le) (rate(flechtwerk_poll_cycle_seconds_bucket{example=~"$example", stage=~"$stage", client_id=~"$client_id"}[5m])))

panels.description: Ownership tokens (config-partition leases) currently held per extractor instance — 0 on a running pod means it sits hot standby. The extractor-side counterpart of transformer task assignment.
panels.title: Ownership tokens — by instance

panels.description: flechtwerk_mqtt_messages_dropped_total{reason="poison"}: the relay raised and the message was ACKed and discarded — unrecoverable data loss. Any value > 0 turns the tile red. Grouped by datasource so you see which source lost data. A red tile is triage, not a pager — pair with an Alertmanager rule.
panels.title: MQTT poison drops — DATA LOSS

panels.description: flechtwerk_mqtt_disconnects_total: unexpected MQTT disconnects (clean shutdown is not counted). There is no in-process reconnect, so any value > 0 is a drop that surfaced as ConnectionError → crash → K8s restart. increase() over the range so the event stays lit after the restart resets the counter.
panels.title: MQTT unexpected disconnects

panels.description: flechtwerk_mqtt_connects_total: one connect per process lifetime is healthy; ≥ 2 over the range (amber) means the MQTT session reconnected — churn or a crash-loop. A single planned deploy legitimately reads 2 (old pod + new pod within the window).
panels.title: MQTT (re)connects — session churn

panels.description: Inbound MQTT rate (arrivals) and the by-design filtered-drop rate on one axis, both as ops/s over $__rate_interval — the gap between the two lines is what the relay forwards to Kafka. They track closely because most Xovis frames carry no counting event and are filtered. Poison drops (data loss) are the red DATA LOSS tile above, not shown here.
panels.title: MQTT messages — arrivals vs filtered (ops/s)

panels.description: flechtwerk_mqtt_buffered_messages: messages left buffered for a subscription after the last drain. A rising, non-draining line is backpressure (the extractor's poll can't keep up) or a stale, never-unsubscribed subscription filling the session inflight window. Read against Poll cycle p95.
panels.title: MQTT buffered messages — by topic (backpressure)

panels.description: Entries currently held in the per-process config store (latest config per wire key). The "did my config arrive?" gauge: after writing a config record, watch this go up; a tombstone takes it down.
panels.title: Config store entries — by client

panels.description: Records drained from config topics into the config store after bootstrap. Config writes are rare, so this shows 5-minute increases rather than a rate — spikes mark config changes landing.
panels.title: Config updates consumed — by topic (5m increase)

panels.description: Entries surviving the startup bootstrap compaction of the config store. Spikes mark stage (re)starts; a restart whose spike is lower than the store-entries gauge expects means configs were tombstoned in between.
panels.title: Config store bootstrap — entries restored at startup (5m increase)

panels.description: Records returned by a single getmany() call.

targets.expr: histogram_quantile(0.5, sum by (client_id, le) (rate(flechtwerk_batch_size_bucket{example=~"$example", stage=~"$stage", client_id=~"$client_id"}[5m])))
histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (client_id, le) (rate(flechtwerk_batch_size_bucket{example=~"$example", stage=~"$stage", client_id=~"$client_id"}[5m])))

panels.title: Batch size — p50 / p95

panels.description: Tasks (input partitions) currently owned and initialized per instance. Drops to 0 and back mark rebalances; a persistent 0 on a running pod means it sits idle (more instances than partitions).
panels.title: Tasks assigned — by instance

panels.description: Changelog records replayed into a task's local state store on initialization. Activity here means rebalances or restarts; per-partition volume shows how expensive each task's restore is.
panels.title: State restored — changelog records replayed (5m increase, by partition)

panels.description: Python GC object churn per stage process — the closest memory-pressure signal for host-run stages (no cAdvisor / process RSS on macOS).
panels.title: GC objects collected / sec — by instance

panels.description: Seconds spent in user code per wall-clock second (rate of flechtwerk_message_processing_seconds_sum) — a CPU-utilization proxy; host-run stages expose no process_cpu_seconds_total on macOS.
panels.title: User-code busy time — fraction of wall clock

panels.description: Python garbage-collection runs per second, per stage process.
panels.title: GC collections / sec — by instance
